ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.

## Self-correction

- Write complete, valid PostgreSQL dbt models before finishing — do not rely on mid-run dbt checks.
- The orchestrator runs dbt after you stop; if models fail, it will ask you to fix SQL in a follow-up pass.
- When you change one model (e.g. casting or date logic), apply the same fix to every model that uses the same pattern.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 21, 11, 48, 373219, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_master_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_master_v2_src"."master_assets" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_master_v2_src.master_assets  (800 rows)

Columns:
- `asset_kennung` text NOT NULL
- `asset_name` text NULL
- `serien_nummer` text NULL
- `garantieende` text NULL
- `kunden_kennung` text NULL
- `projekt_kennung` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_master_v2_src.master_kontakte  (403 rows)

Columns:
- `kontakt_id` text NOT NULL
- `rufname` text NULL
- `familienname` text NULL
- `kontakt_email` text NULL
- `tel` text NULL
- `berufsbezeichnung` text NULL
- `rolle` text NULL
- `korrespondenzsprache` text NULL
- `kd_nummer` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_master_v2_src.master_kunden  (210 rows)

Columns:
- `kundennummer` text NOT NULL
- `unternehmensname` text NULL
- `erp_nr` text NULL
- `kundenklasse` text NULL
- `vertriebsgebiet` text NULL
- `industrie` text NULL
- `homepage` text NULL
- `stadt` text NULL
- `land_region` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_master_v2_src.master_opportunities  (540 rows)

Columns:
- `opp_kennung` text NOT NULL
- `titel` text NULL
- `vertriebsphase` text NULL
- `zieldatum` text NULL
- `auftragswert` text NULL
- `waehrungscode` text NULL
- `kunden_ref` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_master_v2_src.master_projekte  (317 rows)

Columns:
- `projekt_kennung` text NOT NULL
- `projektname` text NULL
- `projektstatus` text NULL
- `go_live_datum` text NULL
- `kunden_kennung` text NULL
- `opp_kennung_ref` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

# Target schema


## Account
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `ERP_Number__c` text
- `Customer_Tier__c` text in (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum)
- `Region__c` text
- `Industry` text
- `Website` text
- `BillingCity` text
- `BillingCountry` text
- `Legacy_Customer_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Contact
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `FirstName` text
- `LastName` text NOT NULL
- `Email` text
- `Phone` text
- `Title` text
- `Role__c` text in (Decision Maker, End User, Technical Contact, Executive Sponsor)
- `Preferred_Language__c` text in (DE, EN, FR, ES, IT)
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Contact_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Opportunity
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `StageName` text NOT NULL in (Prospecting, Qualification, Needs Analysis, Value Proposition, Id. Decision Makers, Perception Analysis, Proposal/Price Quote, Negotiation/Review, Closed Won, Closed Lost)
- `CloseDate` text NOT NULL
- `Amount` double precision
- `CurrencyIsoCode` text
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Project__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Project_Status__c` text in (Active, Completed, In Planning, On Hold, Cancelled)
- `Go_Live_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Opportunity__c` text
- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.

ModelResponse(parts=[ToolCallPart(tool_name='write_file', args={'content': '{{
  config(materialized='table')
}}

SELECT
    MD5(TRIM(opp_kennung)) AS "Id",
    COALESCE(TRIM(titel), 'Unknown Opportunity') AS "Name",
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(vertriebsphase)) IN ('prospecting', 'prospect', 'in kontakt') THEN 'Prospecting'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(vertriebsphase)) IN ('qualification', 'qualifikation', 'quali') THEN 'Qualification'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(vertriebsphase)) = 'in prüfung' THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(vertriebsphase)) IN ('won', 'gewonnen', 'closed won', 'abgeschlossen (gewonnen)') THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(vertriebsphase)) IN ('lost', 'verloren', 'closed lost', 'abgeschlossen (verloren)') THEN 'Closed Lost'
        ELSE 'Prospecting' -- Default for NOT NULL StageName
    END AS "StageName",
    COALESCE(
        CASE
            WHEN TRIM(zieldatum) ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN TO_DATE(TRIM(zieldatum), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
            WHEN TRIM(zieldatum) ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(TRIM(zieldatum), 'DD.MM.YYYY')
            WHEN TRIM(zieldatum) ~ '^\d{1,2}/\d{1,2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(TRIM(zieldatum), 'MM/DD/YYYY')
            WHEN TRIM(zieldatum) ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_DATE(TRIM(zieldatum), 'YYYYMMDD')
            ELSE NULL
        END,
        CURRENT_DATE
    )::TEXT AS "CloseDate",
    CASE
        WHEN TRIM(auftragswert) IS NULL OR TRIM(auftragswert) = '' THEN NULL
        ELSE CAST(REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(auftragswert), '[^0-9.,-]+', '', 'g') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
    END AS "Amount",
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(waehrungscode)) IN ('eur', 'euro', '€') THEN 'EUR'

SELECT
    MD5(TRIM(opp_kennung)) AS "Id",
    COALESCE(TRIM(titel), 'Unknown Opportunity') AS "Name",
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(vertriebsphase)) IN ('prospecting', 'prospect', 'in kontakt') THEN 'Prospecting'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(vertriebsphase)) IN ('qualification', 'qualifikation', 'quali') THEN 'Qualification'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(vertriebsphase)) = 'in prüfung' THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(vertriebsphase)) IN ('won', 'gewonnen', 'closed won', 'abgeschlossen (gewonnen)') THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(vertriebsphase)) IN ('lost', 'verloren', 'closed lost', 'abgeschlossen (verloren)') THEN 'Closed Lost'
        ELSE 'Prospecting' -- Default for NOT NULL StageName
    END AS "StageName",
    COALESCE(
        CASE
            WHEN TRIM(zieldatum) ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN TO_DATE(TRIM(zieldatum), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
            WHEN TRIM(zieldatum) ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(TRIM(zieldatum), 'DD.MM.YYYY')
            WHEN TRIM(zieldatum) ~ '^\d{1,2}/\d{1,2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(TRIM(zieldatum), 'MM/DD/YYYY')
            WHEN TRIM(zieldatum) ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_DATE(TRIM(zieldatum), 'YYYYMMDD')
            ELSE NULL
        END,
        CURRENT_DATE
    )::TEXT AS "CloseDate",
    CASE
        WHEN TRIM(auftragswert) IS NULL OR TRIM(auftragswert) = '' THEN NULL
        ELSE
            (CASE
                WHEN REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(auftragswert), '[^0-9.,-]+', '', 'g') LIKE '%.%,' THEN -- European format like 1.234,56
                    REPLACE(REPLACE(REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(auftragswert), '[^0-9.,-]+', '', 'g'), '.', ''), ',', '.')
                WHEN REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(auftragswert), '[^0-9.,-]+', '', 'g') LIKE '%,%.' THEN -- US format like 1,234.56
                    REPLACE(REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(auftragswert), '[^0-9.,-]+', '', 'g'), ',', '')
                WHEN REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(auftragswert), '[^0-9.,-]+', '', 'g') LIKE '%,%' THEN -- Only comma, assume European decimal like 123,45
                    REPLACE(REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(auftragswert), '[^0-9.,-]+', '', 'g'), ',', '.')
                ELSE -- Only dot, or no separator, assume US decimal
                    REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(auftragswert), '[^0-9.-]+', '', 'g')
            END)::DOUBLE PRECISION
    END AS "Amount",
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(waehrungscode)) IN ('eur', 'euro', '€') THEN 'EUR'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(waehrungscode)) IN ('usd', '$', 'dollar') THEN 'USD'

        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(waehrungscode)) IN ('gbp', '£') THEN 'GBP'
        ELSE NULL

    TRIM(opp_kennung) AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    NULL::TEXT AS "CreatedDate",

FROM

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.

## Self-correction

- Write complete, valid PostgreSQL dbt models before finishing — do not rely on mid-run dbt checks.
- The orchestrator runs dbt after you stop; if models fail, it will ask you to fix SQL in a follow-up pass.
- When you change one model (e.g. casting or date logic), apply the same fix to every model that uses the same pattern.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 21, 11, 48, 373219, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_master_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_master_v2_src"."master_assets" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_master_v2_src.master_assets  (800 rows)

Columns:
- `asset_kennung` text NOT NULL
- `asset_name` text NULL
- `serien_nummer` text NULL
- `garantieende` text NULL
- `kunden_kennung` text NULL
- `projekt_kennung` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_master_v2_src.master_kontakte  (403 rows)

Columns:
- `kontakt_id` text NOT NULL
- `rufname` text NULL
- `familienname` text NULL
- `kontakt_email` text NULL
- `tel` text NULL
- `berufsbezeichnung` text NULL
- `rolle` text NULL
- `korrespondenzsprache` text NULL
- `kd_nummer` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_master_v2_src.master_kunden  (210 rows)

Columns:
- `kundennummer` text NOT NULL
- `unternehmensname` text NULL
- `erp_nr` text NULL
- `kundenklasse` text NULL
- `vertriebsgebiet` text NULL
- `industrie` text NULL
- `homepage` text NULL
- `stadt` text NULL
- `land_region` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_master_v2_src.master_opportunities  (540 rows)

Columns:
- `opp_kennung` text NOT NULL
- `titel` text NULL
- `vertriebsphase` text NULL
- `zieldatum` text NULL
- `auftragswert` text NULL
- `waehrungscode` text NULL
- `kunden_ref` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_master_v2_src.master_projekte  (317 rows)

Columns:
- `projekt_kennung` text NOT NULL
- `projektname` text NULL
- `projektstatus` text NULL
- `go_live_datum` text NULL
- `kunden_kennung` text NULL
- `opp_kennung_ref` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

# Target schema


## Account
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `ERP_Number__c` text
- `Customer_Tier__c` text in (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum)
- `Region__c` text
- `Industry` text
- `Website` text
- `BillingCity` text
- `BillingCountry` text
- `Legacy_Customer_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Contact
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `FirstName` text
- `LastName` text NOT NULL
- `Email` text
- `Phone` text
- `Title` text
- `Role__c` text in (Decision Maker, End User, Technical Contact, Executive Sponsor)
- `Preferred_Language__c` text in (DE, EN, FR, ES, IT)
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Contact_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Opportunity
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `StageName` text NOT NULL in (Prospecting, Qualification, Needs Analysis, Value Proposition, Id. Decision Makers, Perception Analysis, Proposal/Price Quote, Negotiation/Review, Closed Won, Closed Lost)
- `CloseDate` text NOT NULL
- `Amount` double precision
- `CurrencyIsoCode` text
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Project__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Project_Status__c` text in (Active, Completed, In Planning, On Hold, Cancelled)
- `Go_Live_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Opportunity__c` text
- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.


Produce the dbt project that transforms the source into the target. Write one model file per target table listed above — all tables are required.

Self-correction is ON: write complete models; the orchestrator will run dbt after you finish and prompt you to fix failures if needed.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 21, 11, 48, 373226, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 21, 11, 48, 373368, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f3945-df94-7587-a40d-862d16588b24', conversation_id='019f3945-df94-7587-a40d-862cb16f163b')

ModelResponse(parts=[ToolCallPart(tool_name='write_file', args={'path': 'dbt_project/models/sources.yml', 'content': 'version: 2

sources:
  - name: fixture_master_v2_src
    schema: "fixture_master_v2_src"
    tables:
      - name: master_assets
      - name: master_kontakte
      - name: master_kunden

ModelResponse(parts=[ToolCallPart(tool_name='write_file', args={'content': '{{
  config(materialized='table')
}}

SELECT
    MD5(TRIM(opp_kennung)) AS "Id",
    COALESCE(TRIM(titel), 'Unknown Opportunity') AS "Name",
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(vertriebsphase)) IN ('prospecting', 'prospect', 'in kontakt') THEN 'Prospecting'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(vertriebsphase)) IN ('qualification', 'qualifikation', 'quali') THEN 'Qualification'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(vertriebsphase)) = 'in prüfung' THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(vertriebsphase)) IN ('won', 'gewonnen', 'closed won', 'abgeschlossen (gewonnen)') THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(vertriebsphase)) IN ('lost', 'verloren', 'closed lost', 'abgeschlossen (verloren)') THEN 'Closed Lost'
        ELSE 'Prospecting' -- Default for NOT NULL StageName
    END AS "StageName",
    COALESCE(
        CASE
            WHEN TRIM(zieldatum) ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN TO_DATE(TRIM(zieldatum), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
            WHEN TRIM(zieldatum) ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(TRIM(zieldatum), 'DD.MM.YYYY')
            WHEN TRIM(zieldatum) ~ '^\d{1,2}/\d{1,2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(TRIM(zieldatum), 'MM/DD/YYYY')
            WHEN TRIM(zieldatum) ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_DATE(TRIM(zieldatum), 'YYYYMMDD')
            ELSE NULL
        END,
        CURRENT_DATE
    )::TEXT AS "CloseDate",
    CASE
        WHEN TRIM(auftragswert) IS NULL OR TRIM(auftragswert) = '' THEN NULL
        ELSE CAST(REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(auftragswert), '[^0-9.,-]+', '', 'g') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
    END AS "Amount",
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(waehrungscode)) IN ('eur', 'euro', '€') THEN 'EUR'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(waehrungscode)) IN ('usd', '$', 'dollar') THEN 'USD'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(waehrungscode)) IN ('chf', 'sfr') THEN 'CHF' -- Added sfr for completeness if needed
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(waehrungscode)) IN ('gbp', '£') THEN 'GBP'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    MD5(TRIM(kunden_ref)) AS "AccountId",
    TRIM(opp_kennung) AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    NULL::TEXT AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL::TEXT AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM
    {{ source('fixture_master_v2_src', 'master_opportunities') }}

SELECT

    CASE

        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(vertriebsphase)) IN ('qualification', 'qualifikation', 'quali') THEN 'Qualification'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(vertriebsphase)) = 'in prüfung' THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(vertriebsphase)) IN ('won', 'gewonnen', 'closed won', 'abgeschlossen (gewonnen)') THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(vertriebsphase)) IN ('lost', 'verloren', 'closed lost', 'abgeschlossen (verloren)') THEN 'Closed Lost'
        ELSE 'Prospecting' -- Default for NOT NULL StageName
    END AS "StageName",
    COALESCE(
        CASE
            WHEN TRIM(zieldatum) ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN TO_DATE(TRIM(zieldatum), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
            WHEN TRIM(zieldatum) ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(TRIM(zieldatum), 'DD.MM.YYYY')
            WHEN TRIM(zieldatum) ~ '^\d{1,2}/\d{1,2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(TRIM(zieldatum), 'MM/DD/YYYY')
            WHEN TRIM(zieldatum) ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_DATE(TRIM(zieldatum), 'YYYYMMDD')
            ELSE NULL
        END,
        CURRENT_DATE
    )::TEXT AS "CloseDate",
    CASE
        WHEN TRIM(auftragswert) IS NULL OR TRIM(auftragswert) = '' THEN NULL
        ELSE
            (CASE
                WHEN REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(auftragswert), '[^0-9.,-]+', '', 'g') LIKE '%.%,' THEN -- European format like 1.234,56
                    REPLACE(REPLACE(REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(auftragswert), '[^0-9.,-]+', '', 'g'), '.', ''), ',', '.')
                WHEN REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(auftragswert), '[^0-9.,-]+', '', 'g') LIKE '%,%.' THEN -- US format like 1,234.56
                    REPLACE(REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(auftragswert), '[^0-9.,-]+', '', 'g'), ',', '')
                WHEN REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(auftragswert), '[^0-9.,-]+', '', 'g') LIKE '%,%' THEN -- Only comma, assume European decimal like 123,45
                    REPLACE(REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(auftragswert), '[^0-9.,-]+', '', 'g'), ',', '.')
                ELSE -- Only dot, or no separator, assume US decimal
                    REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(auftragswert), '[^0-9.-]+', '', 'g')

    END AS "Amount",
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(waehrungscode)) IN ('eur', 'euro', '€') THEN 'EUR'

    END AS "CurrencyIsoCode",

    TRIM(opp_kennung) AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    NULL::TEXT AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL::TEXT AS "LastModifiedDate",

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.

## Self-correction

- Write complete, valid PostgreSQL dbt models before finishing — do not rely on mid-run dbt checks.
- The orchestrator runs dbt after you stop; if models fail, it will ask you to fix SQL in a follow-up pass.
- When you change one model (e.g. casting or date logic), apply the same fix to every model that uses the same pattern.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 21, 11, 48, 373219, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_master_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_master_v2_src"."master_assets" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_master_v2_src.master_assets  (800 rows)

Columns:
- `asset_kennung` text NOT NULL
- `asset_name` text NULL
- `serien_nummer` text NULL
- `garantieende` text NULL
- `kunden_kennung` text NULL
- `projekt_kennung` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_master_v2_src.master_kontakte  (403 rows)

Columns:
- `kontakt_id` text NOT NULL
- `rufname` text NULL
- `familienname` text NULL
- `kontakt_email` text NULL
- `tel` text NULL
- `berufsbezeichnung` text NULL
- `rolle` text NULL
- `korrespondenzsprache` text NULL
- `kd_nummer` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_master_v2_src.master_kunden  (210 rows)

Columns:
- `kundennummer` text NOT NULL
- `unternehmensname` text NULL
- `erp_nr` text NULL
- `kundenklasse` text NULL
- `vertriebsgebiet` text NULL
- `industrie` text NULL
- `homepage` text NULL
- `stadt` text NULL
- `land_region` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_master_v2_src.master_opportunities  (540 rows)

Columns:
- `opp_kennung` text NOT NULL
- `titel` text NULL
- `vertriebsphase` text NULL
- `zieldatum` text NULL
- `auftragswert` text NULL
- `waehrungscode` text NULL
- `kunden_ref` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_master_v2_src.master_projekte  (317 rows)

Columns:
- `projekt_kennung` text NOT NULL
- `projektname` text NULL
- `projektstatus` text NULL
- `go_live_datum` text NULL
- `kunden_kennung` text NULL
- `opp_kennung_ref` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

# Target schema


## Account
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `ERP_Number__c` text
- `Customer_Tier__c` text in (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum)
- `Region__c` text
- `Industry` text
- `Website` text
- `BillingCity` text
- `BillingCountry` text
- `Legacy_Customer_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Contact
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `FirstName` text
- `LastName` text NOT NULL
- `Email` text
- `Phone` text
- `Title` text
- `Role__c` text in (Decision Maker, End User, Technical Contact, Executive Sponsor)
- `Preferred_Language__c` text in (DE, EN, FR, ES, IT)
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Contact_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Opportunity
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `StageName` text NOT NULL in (Prospecting, Qualification, Needs Analysis, Value Proposition, Id. Decision Makers, Perception Analysis, Proposal/Price Quote, Negotiation/Review, Closed Won, Closed Lost)
- `CloseDate` text NOT NULL
- `Amount` double precision
- `CurrencyIsoCode` text
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Project__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Project_Status__c` text in (Active, Completed, In Planning, On Hold, Cancelled)
- `Go_Live_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Opportunity__c` text
- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.

ModelResponse(parts=[ToolCallPart(tool_name='write_file', args={'content': '{{
  config(materialized='table')
}}

SELECT
    MD5(TRIM(opp_kennung)) AS "Id",
    COALESCE(TRIM(titel), 'Unknown Opportunity') AS "Name",
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(vertriebsphase)) IN ('prospecting', 'prospect', 'in kontakt') THEN 'Prospecting'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(vertriebsphase)) IN ('qualification', 'qualifikation', 'quali') THEN 'Qualification'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(vertriebsphase)) = 'in prüfung' THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(vertriebsphase)) IN ('won', 'gewonnen', 'closed won', 'abgeschlossen (gewonnen)') THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(vertriebsphase)) IN ('lost', 'verloren', 'closed lost', 'abgeschlossen (verloren)') THEN 'Closed Lost'
        ELSE 'Prospecting' -- Default for NOT NULL StageName
    END AS "StageName",
    COALESCE(
        CASE
            WHEN TRIM(zieldatum) ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN TO_DATE(TRIM(zieldatum), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
            WHEN TRIM(zieldatum) ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(TRIM(zieldatum), 'DD.MM.YYYY')
            WHEN TRIM(zieldatum) ~ '^\d{1,2}/\d{1,2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(TRIM(zieldatum), 'MM/DD/YYYY')
            WHEN TRIM(zieldatum) ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_DATE(TRIM(zieldatum), 'YYYYMMDD')
            ELSE NULL
        END,
        CURRENT_DATE
    )::TEXT AS "CloseDate",
    CASE
        WHEN TRIM(auftragswert) IS NULL OR TRIM(auftragswert) = '' THEN NULL
        ELSE CAST(REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(auftragswert), '[^0-9.,-]+', '', 'g') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
    END AS "Amount",
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(waehrungscode)) IN ('eur', 'euro', '€') THEN 'EUR'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(waehrungscode)) IN ('usd', '$', 'dollar') THEN 'USD'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(waehrungscode)) IN ('chf', 'sfr') THEN 'CHF' -- Added sfr for completeness if needed
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(waehrungscode)) IN ('gbp', '£') THEN 'GBP'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    MD5(TRIM(kunden_ref)) AS "AccountId",
    TRIM(opp_kennung) AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    NULL::TEXT AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL::TEXT AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"

        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(vertriebsphase)) IN ('qualification', 'qualifikation', 'quali') THEN 'Qualification'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(vertriebsphase)) = 'in prüfung' THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(vertriebsphase)) IN ('won', 'gewonnen', 'closed won', 'abgeschlossen (gewonnen)') THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(vertriebsphase)) IN ('lost', 'verloren', 'closed lost', 'abgeschlossen (verloren)') THEN 'Closed Lost'
        ELSE 'Prospecting' -- Default for NOT NULL StageName
    END AS "StageName",
    COALESCE(
        CASE
            WHEN TRIM(zieldatum) ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN TO_DATE(TRIM(zieldatum), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
            WHEN TRIM(zieldatum) ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(TRIM(zieldatum), 'DD.MM.YYYY')
            WHEN TRIM(zieldatum) ~ '^\d{1,2}/\d{1,2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(TRIM(zieldatum), 'MM/DD/YYYY')
            WHEN TRIM(zieldatum) ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_DATE(TRIM(zieldatum), 'YYYYMMDD')
            ELSE NULL
        END,
        CURRENT_DATE
    )::TEXT AS "CloseDate",
    CASE
        WHEN TRIM(auftragswert) IS NULL OR TRIM(auftragswert) = '' THEN NULL
        ELSE
            (CASE
                WHEN REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(auftragswert), '[^0-9.,-]+', '', 'g') LIKE '%.%,' THEN -- European format like 1.234,56
                    REPLACE(REPLACE(REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(auftragswert), '[^0-9.,-]+', '', 'g'), '.', ''), ',', '.')
                WHEN REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(auftragswert), '[^0-9.,-]+', '', 'g') LIKE '%,%.' THEN -- US format like 1,234.56
                    REPLACE(REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(auftragswert), '[^0-9.,-]+', '', 'g'), ',', '')
                WHEN REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(auftragswert), '[^0-9.,-]+', '', 'g') LIKE '%,%' THEN -- Only comma, assume European decimal like 123,45
                    REPLACE(REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(auftragswert), '[^0-9.,-]+', '', 'g'), ',', '.')
                ELSE -- Only dot, or no separator, assume US decimal
                    REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(auftragswert), '[^0-9.-]+', '', 'g')
            END)::DOUBLE PRECISION
    END AS "Amount",
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(waehrungscode)) IN ('eur', 'euro', '€') THEN 'EUR'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(waehrungscode)) IN ('usd', '$', 'dollar') THEN 'USD'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(waehrungscode)) IN ('chf', 'sfr') THEN 'CHF' -- Added sfr for completeness if needed
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(waehrungscode)) IN ('gbp', '£') THEN 'GBP'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    MD5(TRIM(kunden_ref)) AS "AccountId",
    TRIM(opp_kennung) AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    NULL::TEXT AS "CreatedDate",

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.

## Self-correction

- Write complete, valid PostgreSQL dbt models before finishing — do not rely on mid-run dbt checks.
- The orchestrator runs dbt after you stop; if models fail, it will ask you to fix SQL in a follow-up pass.
- When you change one model (e.g. casting or date logic), apply the same fix to every model that uses the same pattern.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 21, 11, 48, 373219, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_master_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_master_v2_src"."master_assets" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_master_v2_src.master_assets  (800 rows)

Columns:
- `asset_kennung` text NOT NULL
- `asset_name` text NULL
- `serien_nummer` text NULL
- `garantieende` text NULL
- `kunden_kennung` text NULL
- `projekt_kennung` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_master_v2_src.master_kontakte  (403 rows)

Columns:
- `kontakt_id` text NOT NULL
- `rufname` text NULL
- `familienname` text NULL
- `kontakt_email` text NULL
- `tel` text NULL
- `berufsbezeichnung` text NULL
- `rolle` text NULL
- `korrespondenzsprache` text NULL
- `kd_nummer` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_master_v2_src.master_kunden  (210 rows)

Columns:
- `kundennummer` text NOT NULL
- `unternehmensname` text NULL
- `erp_nr` text NULL
- `kundenklasse` text NULL
- `vertriebsgebiet` text NULL
- `industrie` text NULL
- `homepage` text NULL
- `stadt` text NULL
- `land_region` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_master_v2_src.master_opportunities  (540 rows)

Columns:
- `opp_kennung` text NOT NULL
- `titel` text NULL
- `vertriebsphase` text NULL
- `zieldatum` text NULL
- `auftragswert` text NULL
- `waehrungscode` text NULL
- `kunden_ref` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_master_v2_src.master_projekte  (317 rows)

Columns:
- `projekt_kennung` text NOT NULL
- `projektname` text NULL
- `projektstatus` text NULL
- `go_live_datum` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

# Target schema


## Account
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `ERP_Number__c` text
- `Customer_Tier__c` text in (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum)
- `Region__c` text
- `Industry` text
- `Website` text
- `BillingCity` text
- `BillingCountry` text
- `Legacy_Customer_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Contact
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `FirstName` text
- `LastName` text NOT NULL
- `Email` text

- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.

        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(vertriebsphase)) IN ('prospecting', 'prospect', 'in kontakt') THEN 'Prospecting'

        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(vertriebsphase)) = 'in prüfung' THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(vertriebsphase)) IN ('won', 'gewonnen', 'closed won', 'abgeschlossen (gewonnen)') THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(vertriebsphase)) IN ('lost', 'verloren', 'closed lost', 'abgeschlossen (verloren)') THEN 'Closed Lost'
        ELSE 'Prospecting' -- Default for NOT NULL StageName
    END AS "StageName",

        CASE
            WHEN TRIM(zieldatum) ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN TO_DATE(TRIM(zieldatum), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
            WHEN TRIM(zieldatum) ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(TRIM(zieldatum), 'DD.MM.YYYY')

    END AS "Amount",
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(waehrungscode)) IN ('eur', 'euro', '€') THEN 'EUR'

        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(waehrungscode)) IN ('chf', 'sfr') THEN 'CHF' -- Added sfr for completeness if needed
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(waehrungscode)) IN ('gbp', '£') THEN 'GBP'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    MD5(TRIM(kunden_ref)) AS "AccountId",
    TRIM(opp_kennung) AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    NULL::TEXT AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL::TEXT AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM

messages.raw:
ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. ModelRequest(parts=[ToolReturnPart(tool_name='read_file', content='{{
  config(materialized='table')
}}

SELECT
    MD5(TRIM(opp_kennung)) AS "Id",
    COALESCE(TRIM(titel), 'Unknown Opportunity') AS "Name",
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(vertriebsphase)) IN ('prospecting', 'prospect', 'in kontakt') THEN 'Prospecting'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(vertriebsphase)) IN ('qualification', 'qualifikation', 'quali') THEN 'Qualification'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(vertriebsphase)) = 'in prüfung' THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(vertriebsphase)) IN ('won', 'gewonnen', 'closed won', 'abgeschlossen (gewonnen)') THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(vertriebsphase)) IN ('lost', 'verloren', 'closed lost', 'abgeschlossen (verloren)') THEN 'Closed Lost'
        ELSE 'Prospecting' -- Default for NOT NULL StageName
    END AS "StageName",
    COALESCE(
        CASE
            WHEN TRIM(zieldatum) ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN TO_DATE(TRIM(zieldatum), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
            WHEN TRIM(zieldatum) ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(TRIM(zieldatum), 'DD.MM.YYYY')
            WHEN TRIM(zieldatum) ~ '^\d{1,2}/\d{1,2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(TRIM(zieldatum), 'MM/DD/YYYY')
            WHEN TRIM(zieldatum) ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_DATE(TRIM(zieldatum), 'YYYYMMDD')
            ELSE NULL
        END,
        CURRENT_DATE
    )::TEXT AS "CloseDate",
    CASE
        WHEN TRIM(auftragswert) IS NULL OR TRIM(auftragswert) = '' THEN NULL
        ELSE
            (CASE
                WHEN REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(auftragswert), '[^0-9.,-]+', '', 'g') LIKE '%.%,' THEN -- European format like 1.234,56
                    REPLACE(REPLACE(REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(auftragswert), '[^0-9.,-]+', '', 'g'), '.', ''), ',', '.')
                WHEN REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(auftragswert), '[^0-9.,-]+', '', 'g') LIKE '%,%.' THEN -- US format like 1,234.56
                    REPLACE(REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(auftragswert), '[^0-9.,-]+', '', 'g'), ',', '')
                WHEN REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(auftragswert), '[^0-9.,-]+', '', 'g') LIKE '%,%' THEN -- Only comma, assume European decimal like 123,45
                    REPLACE(REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(auftragswert), '[^0-9.,-]+', '', 'g'), ',', '.')
                ELSE -- Only dot, or no separator, assume US decimal
                    REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(auftragswert), '[^0-9.-]+', '', 'g')
            END)::DOUBLE PRECISION
    END AS "Amount",
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(waehrungscode)) IN ('eur', 'euro', '€') THEN 'EUR'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(waehrungscode)) IN ('usd', '$', 'dollar') THEN 'USD'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(waehrungscode)) IN ('chf', 'sfr') THEN 'CHF' -- Added sfr for completeness if needed
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(waehrungscode)) IN ('gbp', '£') THEN 'GBP'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    MD5(TRIM(kunden_ref)) AS "AccountId",
    TRIM(opp_kennung) AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    NULL::TEXT AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL::TEXT AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM
    {{ source('fixture_master_v2_src', 'master_opportunities') }}
', tool_call_id='pyd_ai_e82082c0d19143138daa57a110c17d30', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 21, 13, 0, 686175, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 21, 13, 0, 686368, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f3946-f52c-7294-a972-13ae117cf575', conversation_id='019f3946-f52c-7294-a972-13add607d2ef')

The `REGEXP_REPLACE` and `REPLACE` functions, if applied to a string containing only non-numeric characters, could result in an empty string, which then fails when cast to `DOUBLE PRECISION`.

To fix this, I will add an explicit check to ensure that the cleaned string for `Amount` is not empty before attempting the `DOUBLE PRECISION` cast. If it's an empty string after cleaning, it will be treated as `NULL`.

I will rewrite the `Amount` column logic to first clean the string and then check if the cleaned string is empty before casting.

Here's the revised `models/Opportunity.sql`:
', provider_name='google-cloud', provider_details={'thought_signature': ''}), ToolCallPart(tool_name='write_file', args={'content': '{{

        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(vertriebsphase)) = 'in prüfung' THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(vertriebsphase)) IN ('won', 'gewonnen', 'closed won', 'abgeschlossen (gewonnen)') THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(vertriebsphase)) IN ('lost', 'verloren', 'closed lost', 'abgeschlossen (verloren)') THEN 'Closed Lost'
        ELSE 'Prospecting' -- Default for NOT NULL StageName
    END AS "StageName",
    COALESCE(
        CASE
            WHEN TRIM(zieldatum) ~ '^\\d{4}-\\d{2}-\\d{2}$' THEN TO_DATE(TRIM(zieldatum), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
            WHEN TRIM(zieldatum) ~ '^\\d{2}\\.\\d{2}\\.\\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(TRIM(zieldatum), 'DD.MM.YYYY')
            WHEN TRIM(zieldatum) ~ '^\\d{1,2}/\\d{1,2}/\\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(TRIM(zieldatum), 'MM/DD/YYYY')
            WHEN TRIM(zieldatum) ~ '^\\d{8}$' THEN TO_DATE(TRIM(zieldatum), 'YYYYMMDD')
            ELSE NULL
        END,
        CURRENT_DATE
    )::TEXT AS "CloseDate",
    CASE
        WHEN TRIM(auftragswert) IS NULL OR TRIM(auftragswert) = '' THEN NULL
        ELSE
            (SELECT

                    WHEN cleaned_amount = '' THEN NULL

                            REPLACE(REPLACE(REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(auftragswert), '[^0-9.,-]+', '', 'g'), '.', ''), ',', '.')
                        WHEN REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(auftragswert), '[^0-9.,-]+', '', 'g') LIKE '%,%.' THEN -- US format like 1,234.56

                        WHEN REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(auftragswert), '[^0-9.,-]+', '', 'g') LIKE '%,%' THEN -- Only comma, assume European decimal like 123,45
                            REPLACE(REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(auftragswert), '[^0-9.,-]+', '', 'g'), ',', '.')
                        ELSE -- Only dot, or no separator, assume US decimal
                            REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(auftragswert), '[^0-9.-]+', '', 'g')
                    END AS cleaned_amount
            ) AS _sub_amount)
    END AS "Amount",
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(waehrungscode)) IN ('eur', 'euro', '€') THEN 'EUR'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(waehrungscode)) IN ('usd', '$', 'dollar') THEN 'USD'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(waehrungscode)) IN ('chf', 'sfr') THEN 'CHF'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(waehrungscode)) IN ('gbp', '£') THEN 'GBP'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    MD5(TRIM(kunden_ref)) AS "AccountId",
    TRIM(opp_kennung) AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    NULL::TEXT AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL::TEXT AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM
    {{ source('fixture_master_v2_src', 'master_opportunities') }}
', 'path': 'models/Opportunity.sql'}, tool_call_id='pyd_ai_56c3f45b38384153b620fbe2313ed795')], usage=RequestUsage(input_tokens=2414, output_tokens=2701, details={'thoughts_tokens': 1485, 'text_prompt_tokens': 2414, 'text_candidates_tokens': 1216}), model_name='gemini-2.5-flash', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 21, 13, 14, 279402, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), provider_name='google-cloud', provider_url='https://aiplatform.googleapis.com/', provider_details={'finish_reason': 'STOP', 'timestamp': datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 21, 13, 0, 796012, tzinfo=TzInfo(0)), 'traffic_type': 'ON_DEMAND'}, provider_response_id='XBpMauzKMI21ur4Pi4qC4AQ', finish_reason='stop')

        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(vertriebsphase)) IN ('won', 'gewonnen', 'closed won', 'abgeschlossen (gewonnen)') THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(vertriebsphase)) IN ('lost', 'verloren', 'closed lost', 'abgeschlossen (verloren)') THEN 'Closed Lost'
        ELSE 'Prospecting' -- Default for NOT NULL StageName
    END AS "StageName",
    COALESCE(
        CASE
            WHEN TRIM(zieldatum) ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN TO_DATE(TRIM(zieldatum), 'YYYY-MM-DD')

            WHEN TRIM(zieldatum) ~ '^\d{1,2}/\d{1,2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(TRIM(zieldatum), 'MM/DD/YYYY')
            WHEN TRIM(zieldatum) ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_DATE(TRIM(zieldatum), 'YYYYMMDD')
            ELSE NULL
        END,
        CURRENT_DATE
    )::TEXT AS "CloseDate",
    CASE
        WHEN TRIM(auftragswert) IS NULL OR TRIM(auftragswert) = '' THEN NULL
        ELSE

                WHEN REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(auftragswert), '[^0-9.,-]+', '', 'g') LIKE '%.%,' THEN -- European format like 1.234,56

                WHEN REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(auftragswert), '[^0-9.,-]+', '', 'g') LIKE '%,%.' THEN -- US format like 1,234.56

                WHEN REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(auftragswert), '[^0-9.,-]+', '', 'g') LIKE '%,%' THEN -- Only comma, assume European decimal like 123,45
                    REPLACE(REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(auftragswert), '[^0-9.,-]+', '', 'g'), ',', '.')

                    REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(auftragswert), '[^0-9.-]+', '', 'g')
            END)::DOUBLE PRECISION
    END AS "Amount",

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.

## Self-correction

- Write complete, valid PostgreSQL dbt models before finishing — do not rely on mid-run dbt checks.
- The orchestrator runs dbt after you stop; if models fail, it will ask you to fix SQL in a follow-up pass.
- When you change one model (e.g. casting or date logic), apply the same fix to every model that uses the same pattern.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 21, 13, 23, 827179, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='dbt run failed after your last edit. Fix every failing model using valid PostgreSQL only (no TRY_CAST or invented functions). read_file each model you change. The orchestrator will re-run dbt automatically.

- Opportunity: Database Error in model Opportunity (models/Opportunity.sql)
  invalid input syntax for type double precision: "400.902.63"
  compiled code at target/run/aidmi_generated/models/Opportunity.sql', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 21, 13, 23, 827184, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 21, 13, 23, 827297, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f3947-5472-7491-8547-adadd4c7c735', conversation_id='019f3947-5472-7491-8547-adac2c2fcc51')

4.  **Default (US format, or no separators):** Any remaining cases will be treated as US format (only `.` or no separators) and only non-numeric characters will be removed.

This revised logic will ensure that "400.902.63" is correctly identified as a European number, transformed into "400902.63", and then successfully cast to `DOUBLE PRECISION`.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.

## Self-correction

- Write complete, valid PostgreSQL dbt models before finishing — do not rely on mid-run dbt checks.
- The orchestrator runs dbt after you stop; if models fail, it will ask you to fix SQL in a follow-up pass.
- When you change one model (e.g. casting or date logic), apply the same fix to every model that uses the same pattern.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 21, 13, 23, 827179, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='dbt run failed after your last edit. Fix every failing model using valid PostgreSQL only (no TRY_CAST or invented functions). read_file each model you change. The orchestrator will re-run dbt automatically.
